title: Sohinee Bera
text:
Hello! I’m a 5th year Ph.D. candidate in the Department of Communication at Cornell University, where I study how structural and cultural conditions shape participation and voice in science, health, environmental, and risk decision-making. I draw on qualitative methods- interviews, participant observation, and textual analysis- along with surveys to explore this across different contexts.
My dissertation follows female community health workers in rural India as they navigate national health policies, data-driven reporting systems, and the everyday needs of the communities they serve. This work sits at the intersection of critical communication approaches, science and technology studies, and national health policy, examining how structural vulnerabilities and data practices shape care on the ground.
My work has been featured in the Cornell Chronicle and ScienceIRL. I have published in the International Journal of Communication, Public Understanding of Science, Ambio: A Journal of Environment and Society, and Frontiers in Communication.
I’ve also been awarded funding from the Waterhouse Family Institute, Cornell’s Qualitative and Interpretive Research Institue, Cornell’s Graduate School, Cornell’s Department of Communication, Cornell’s South Asia Program, and the National Science Foundation.
I am currently on the academic job market for positions beginning Fall 2027 and am seeking out roles where I can contribute to research, teaching, and community engagement.





M.S., PhD in Science, Health, Risk, & Environmental Communication Aug 2022 - Present
Committee Members: Bruce V. Lewenstein, Katherine McComas, Jeff Niederdeppe, Mohan Dutta (Massey University)



B.S. in Agricultural Communication Aug 2017 - Dec 2020

April 2026: Published a co-authored article on “Insights for Inclusive Upstream Engagement with Migrant Farmworkers in Agriculture: A Case Study From the US-Mexico Border” in Ambio: A Journal of Environment and Society
March 2026: Returned from 3.5 months of dissertation fieldwork on female community health workers from rural West Bengal, India
Feburary 2026: Published a co-authored article on “Examining Media Coverage of Ethical Dimensions of Advanced Algorithmic Technology” in Public Understanding of Science

title: Teaching
text: My teaching philosophy is tied to my research in health, science, and environmental communication, beginning with a rejection of the deficit model. The deficit model assumes that experts hold authoritative knowledge, while audiences (or students, in this case) are passive recipients for information. Instead, I approach education as a co-constructive process built on dialogue and mutual learning. My goal is not simply to transmit key concepts, theories, and skills, but to help students develop the tools to think critically, build evidence-based arguments, and understand complex issues from multiple perspectives, whether its navigating a public health controversy, crafting a strategic communication campaign, or evaluating an advertising narrative. With teaching experience from elementary school to the university, I’m passionate about helping students of all ages learn how to think.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching Experience



Fall 2025  Cornell University


Teaching Assistant — Communication and Technology  Supported 60 undergraduates through grading, office hours, and one-on-one meetings; contributed to exam design and managed course email site.




Summer 2025  Cornell University


Instructor of Record — Communication, Environment, Science, and Health  Designed and instructed a 6-week online course for 15 undergraduates, including syllabus development, lecture design and delivery, review sessions, and all grading.




Spring 2024  Lehman Alternative Community School


Teaching Fellow — Whose Knowledge Counts? Navigating the Science, Politics, and Ethics of Saving Our Planet Through Place-Based Knowledge Systems  Co-designed and delivered a 3-lesson curriculum for 10th graders on how knowledge from diverse cultures shapes environmental decision-making; coordinated with classroom teacher to ensure alignment with New York State learning standards and developed interactive, discussion-based lessons.




Summer 2023  Cornell University


Instructor of Record — Communication, Environment, Science, and Health  Designed and instructed a 3-week online course for 5 undergraduates, including syllabus development, lecture design and delivery, review sessions, and all grading.




Spring 2023  Belle Sherman Elementary School


Teaching Fellow — Discovering Nature and Art in Everyday Life  Co-designed and delivered a 3-lesson curriculum for 3rd and 4th graders on basic plant identification, plant pressing, and cyanotype sunprints; coordinated with classroom teacher to ensure alignment with New York State learning standards and developed interactive, discussion-based lessons.




Spring 2023  Cornell University


Teaching Assistant — Communication, Environment, Science, and Health  DSupported 50 undergraduates through grading, office hours, and one-on-one advising; contributed to assignment and exam design and delivered guest lectures.




Fall 2022  Cornell University


Teaching Assistant — Communication and Technology  Supported 55 undergraduates through grading, office hours, and one-on-one meetings; contributed to assignment and exam design.




Fall 2021 - Spring 2022  Geschwister Scholl Realschule Andernach, Germany


U.S. Fulbright Grantee: Teaching Assistant — English  Fostered community and inclusion at a German secondary school with a significant number of students from refugee and migrant backgrounds. Designed and delivered lessons from American culture, history, and media alongside English grammar, vocabulatry, pronunication, and reading and writing skills. Developed an afterschool English club, creating informal spaces for language practice and community building.

title: Research
text:
I first started off in this field interested in a deficit-based question, trying to understand why don’t people do what we expect them to do in science, health, environment, and risk decision-making. Why do people continue to bathe in the toxic, polluted water of the Ganges River? Why do some visitors who love the environment off-road illegally? I quickly came to realize that was the wrong question. Behaviors that look irrational from the outside are often reasonable responses to the structural and cultural conditions that shape them. Which is why attempts at meaningful social change requires understanding these conditions and listening to the communities living within them.
But what does it actually mean to listen? My research is concerned with how participation and voice operate in science, health, risk, and environmental interventions. By that, I mean whose knowledge counts as legitimate, how problems get defined and by whom, who bears responsibility for solutions, and whose experiences of harm are taken seriously. These are not just procedural questions, but questions about power.
My research has investigating these arrangements across a range of contexts: responsible recreation in national parks, responsible agricultural biotechnology development at research centers, and rural health care delivery in India. Across these projects, I use mixed-methods, from survey research, message design and testing, to ethnographic-based observation, interviews, and textual analysis.
What connects these projects is a commitment to treating participation as a structural question, not a performative one. It’s not enough to include communities in the design of interventions. The deeper work is understanding what it takes to actually hear them.

title: Research
text:
Projects


  
    
    
      Off-roading at Berdoo Canyon Road, Joshua Tree National Park
    
  

  
    Community-Centered Off-Road Management in Joshua Tree National Park 
    
      Off-roaders are often framed as a problem in conservation, but that framing misses the fact that these communities have their own deep relationships with land, their own definitions of responsible recreation, and their own sense of what the problems in wilderness areas are. In partnership with Joshua Tree National Park and The Living Desert, we worked to understand off-roaders' perceptions and design conservation messaging that could speak to this community.
    
    
      Grants: California State Parks Off-Highway Motor Vehicle Recreation Grant / $114,625 (Unfunded) / 2024
    
  






  
    
    
      Injecting a plant with a synthetic RUBY gene
    
  

  
    Upstream Engagement in Emerging Agricultural Biotechnologies
    
      As climate change accelerates, research centers are turning to digital and technological solutions to make agricultural systems more sustainable and efficient. But who gets to shape those solutions, and whose priorities get built into them from the start, is rarely asked early enough. This project intervenes at that moment before technologies are developed and distributed, when public input can feedback into research decisions. 
     
      Working with CROPPS  collaborators across Cornell University, the University of Illinois, and the University of Arizona, we engaged a diverse set of stakeholders to understand the ethical and social implications of emerging agricultural biotechnologies and what more responsible, responsive science might look like. This included fieldwork with migrant farmworkers at 2 AM at the U.S.-Mexico border, in partnership with Campesino Sin Fronteras (a local farmworker organization) to understand the particular challenges of including marginalized communities in these conversations. We conducted surveys at the Slow Food Terra Madre international food festival to understand how sustainably-minded consumers, growers, and producers think about these innovations. We also engaged with traditional agricultural stakeholders (plant scientists, large-scale growers). 
    
    
      Papers:
      Insights for inclusive upstream engagement with migrant farmworkers in agriculture: A case study from the US–Mexico border
    
    
      Grants: Science and Technology Center: Center for Research on Programmable Plant Systems / National Science Foundation
    
  






  
    
    
      ASHAs protesting in Janurary 2026 for better pay, worker rights, and recognition
    
  

  
    The Communicative Politics of Community Health Work in Rural India
    
    Community-based health programs have long been championed as a model for meaningful global development and social change. Yet, the communicative arrangements through which those programs operate can reproduce the very logics they claim to disrupt. My dissertation focuses on India's National Health Mission, the world's largest community health program. I examine the data and reporting infrastructures behind the program, which shapes how problems are defined, which outcomes are recorded, and which behaviors are rewarded, to understand health care delivery on the ground. 
    I draw on three months of ethnographic-based fieldwork with female community healthworkers in rural West Bengal, India, including participant observation, semi-structured interviews, as well as textual analyses of policy documents. This dissertation develops the concept of entangled care, in which geniune benefit and harm go hand in hand, empowerment can come at the cost of marginalization, and coercion is interwinted with care. 
    
    
      Funding: 
      Waterhouse Family Institute / $5,600 / 2025
    
        
    
      Cornell's Qualitative and Interpretive Research Institute / $2,000 / 2025
    
    
        
    
    Cornell's Department of Communication / $2,000 / 2025
